ontology.foodex2_description: Pumpkin soup, cream-style, prepared with milk
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for cream of pumpkin soup with whole milk; A0CKP (vegetable cream soup) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cream of pumpkin soup; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of cream of pumpkin soup is approximately 250 g, typical of a starter or light meal serving in a restaurant or home setting.

nutrient_source.title: Cream of Pumpkin Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cream of Pumpkin Soup (F14060005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100g based on whole-milk and butter composition consistent with HPB source profile; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for cream of pumpkin soup found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Pumpkin (boiled, mashed) has a published GI of approximately 75 (high) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The cream soup preparation with whole milk and fat content moderates the glycaemic response somewhat; however, in the absence of a direct tested value for this preparation, GI 75 is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (75 × 7.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 13.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 234 mg/100g; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request low-sodium broth preparation, as a standard 250 g bowl delivers approximately 585 mg sodium, approaching 25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (estimated 75) from pumpkin base, partially offset by fat and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume cream of pumpkin soup in portions of 150 g or less, pair it with a low-GI protein source, and avoid bread or crouton accompaniments to limit the glycaemic load to below 10 g per meal occasion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.5 g/100g from whole milk and butter; sodium 234 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, substitute whole milk with low-fat milk or unsweetened plant milk and use olive oil in place of butter to reduce saturated fat intake, keeping a 250 g serving below the 5 g saturated fat threshold recommended by heart health guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-carotene and antioxidant content from pumpkin; saturated fat below 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cream of pumpkin soup is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to pumpkin's beta-carotene and antioxidant profile; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil further enhances anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cream of Pumpkin Soup is a smooth, velvety pureed soup made from cooked pumpkin blended with whole milk or cream and seasoned vegetable broth. The preparation involves sautéing aromatics such as onion and garlic, simmering diced pumpkin until tender, then blending the mixture to a silky consistency and finishing with whole milk to achieve a creamy texture. The flavour profile is mildly sweet from the pumpkin, subtly savoury from the broth and salt, and gently warming from spices such as nutmeg or white pepper. The texture is thick, smooth, and homogeneous with no visible chunks. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 72 kcal, 1.7 g protein, 4.07 g fat (of which approximately 2.5 g is saturated from the dairy component), and 7.2 g carbohydrate with 1.0 g dietary fibre and 1.3 g sugar. Sodium is 234 mg/100g, placing it in the borderline range for sodium-sensitive individuals. Pumpkin contributes beta-carotene (a vitamin A precursor), vitamin C, and potassium, supporting antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity. The glycaemic index is estimated at 75 (high) based on boiled mashed pumpkin data, though the fat and fibre content of the soup preparation moderates the glycaemic response in practice. This dish is suitable for vegetarians and is gluten-free. It is commonly served as a starter or light meal and is popular in Western and international restaurant menus as well as home cooking across Southeast Asia including Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14060005) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known dairy composition consistent with the source profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled mashed pumpkin as the closest approved reference.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item as of 2026. Saturated fat of 2.5 g/100g is estimated from the known composition of whole milk and butter consistent with the HPB source profile and is not a directly measured value. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on boiled mashed pumpkin (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for cream of pumpkin soup preparation was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (75 × 7.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.5 g. This food is classified as Western/International cuisine and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under G6 rules. All core macros (protein_g 1.7, fat_g 4.07, carbohydrate_g 7.2) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
